dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for a male unresponsive at a residence in [CITY].
Hi, I'm Alex. I'm an EMT. What happened to bring you to 911 today?
He was found unresponsive on his couch. He groaned when touched with shortness of breath.
He has COPD, prostate cancer, and throat cancer. He stopped his home oxygen recently.
Can you tell me your name?
He wasn't speaking when we found him. He opened his eyes slightly to touch.
How long was he unresponsive before the call?
The neighbor found him around 13:50 today.
He's opening his eyes to touch but is confused on initial assessment.
His verbal response is garbled initially but he answers questions coherently after oxygen improves SpO2.
He moved after verbal and physical prompting but is weak when attempting to grab our hand.
Airway is patent. Observed some gag reflex, no signs of obstruction or secretions.
He's more comfortable sitting slightly upright. Positioning him in a semi-Fowler's as recommended.
Breathing is tachypneic, showing accessory muscle use. Chest rise is present but labored.
Respirations at 31/min. Severe hypoxia with room air SpO2 of 36%.
Administering high-flow O2 now due to SpO2 < 90%.
Circulation appears good. Radial pulses are strong with a rate of ~124.
Pulse is 124 bpm, regular initially, with no signs of pulse deficits.
Blood pressure is 123 over 96. We'll need to check it again as there's possible hypotension later.
EKG showing sinus tachycardia with some premature atrial contractions, as per 12-lead.
What is the patient's name and age, please?
He's had mucus in coughs for a few days. Had lunch today at 1 PM via PEG tube.
Known COPD, prostate and throat cancer history. He's unsure about ongoing treatment.
His last intake was around 1 PM. No vomiting or diarrhea reported prior.
He's not known to have any drug or food allergies.
He was found unresponsive, speaking erratically, but no fever, chills, or malodorous urine reported.
On a scale of 0 to 10, would you say your current pain is 0, sir?
No pain from the PEG site or in any abdomen region reported yet.
Skin is warm and dry with normal capillary refill time noted during check-up.
Head is normocephalic and eyes are PERRL with no signs of JVD or DCAP-BTLS.
No nasal or mouth discharge noted, and swallow seems unremarkable.
Coarse rhonchi in the left upper lung zones with decreased lung sounds in the lower fields.
PEG site is clean and free from signs of redness or concern.
No extremity swelling or deformity noted. Positive PMS bilaterally with normal strength and coordination.
No complaints of pain on urination, fever, or changes in urinary habits brought to attention yet.
Glucose is dramatically elevated at 315 mg/dL as per recent testing.
SpO2 increased with O2 support to 88% on 15LPM NRB.
Initiating non-rebreather mask at 15LPM and starting capnography assessment.
Providing 10 mg dexamethasone IV and Duoneb for airway support.
Completing ECG 4 lead monitoring per protocol now for continuous cardiac observation.
Using the respiratory distress/COPD protocol given the tachypnea and hypoxemia.
Reassessing vitals post O2 administration and medication.
BP has decreased to 104/70, pulse still 122, and SpO2 at 88%.
Capnography showing colorimetric indicator consistent with good ventilation attempts.
18G IV in external jugular secured and fluids are administered IV.
ECG shows return to sinus tach with no further PACs observed.
Transporting to [COMPANY_NAME] for hypoxia and low BP control. His hypoxia remains a concern.
Patient secured in semi-Fowler's position with leg and chest supports in place.
Hospital has been given received details about the patient's hypoxia and elevated glucose.
Prepared pre-arrival report with vital signs and assessments to share at ED.
Let them know he was on 4L O2 with an SpO2 of 91% before hospital arrival.
All med interventions logged and ready for seamless transition.
Initial resps were 31, now down to 29. EKG now stable at sinus tachycardia.
His condition is unstable and should be triaged and monitored in ED-R upon arrival.
Understand. We'll closely monitor renal function given the elevated glucose.
Arrived at hospital for care transfer. Preparing patient on new stretcher for cart.
Ensuring he stays warm with an added blanket.
Preparing for handover. Patient has been transferred to ED.
Handoff from our team complete. If needed, monitor for return of hypoxia or confusion.
Appears stable now. Remind ED team he should remain on 4L O2 at least.
Looks calm and alert, no signs of acute distress.
Final SpO2 pre-hospital is 91% on 4L O2 with no distress.
BP has dropped further to 72 over 47, pulse up to 120 per minute.
SpO2 remains at 91% with respiration at 26, stable and sinus.
Respiratory function improved with adequate flow of oxygen and meds.
He also mentioned falls earlier in the week but no significant swelling or edema reported at the time.
